content:
What .kts files actually are, why Gradle’s dependencies { } is just a function call, and how to build a working HTML DSL from scratch

18 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
You’ve seen the term everywhere. “Kotlin DSL.” It’s in your Gradle files, it’s in Jetpack Compose discussions, it’s in every Ktor tutorial. And if you’ve ever asked “okay, but what is it?”, you’ve probably been handed one of two answers: a dictionary definition (“Domain-Specific Language!”) that explains nothing, or a wall of code that assumes you already know.
This article is the missing middle. By the end of it, you will understand exactly what a Kotlin DSL is, what those .kts files are and why Gradle uses them; why code like dependencies { implementation("...") } is perfectly ordinary Kotlin wearing a costume, and how to build your own DSL (a working HTML builder) line by line.
One more acronym before we start. If you’re coming from Android you’ve also been bombarded with KTX (core-ktx, fragment-ktx…). KTX is not the Kotlin DSL, nor anything to do with .kts files, despite sharing letters with both. KTX libraries are extension functions that make individual Android calls nicer; a DSL builds a whole nested vocabulary. Same soil, different plants. Part 2 pins down both.
No prior DSL knowledge required. Let’s settle this once and for all.

Related GitHub Repo:
https://github.com/ioannisa/HtmlBuilder-Kotlin-DSL-Demo
Important:
All the code in this article lives in a companion project linked above.
An Android app that renders the DSL’s output in a live WebView, with an interactive tab where you add products and watch the loop from Part 5 re-render.
The DSL itself sits in a single pure-Kotlin file with zero Android imports, and unit tests verify every output printed below, character for character.

Part 1: What “DSL” Actually Means
A Domain-Specific Language is a language designed for one job, in contrast to a general-purpose language designed for any job.
You already use DSLs every day:

• SQL is a DSL for querying data. You can’t build a mobile app with it, but for asking “give me all users older than 30,” nothing beats it.

• Regex is a DSL for pattern matching in text.

• CSS is a DSL for describing visual styling.

• HTML is a DSL for describing document structure.
None of these can do what Kotlin or Java can. That’s the point. By giving up generality, a DSL can be dramatically more expressive within its domain. SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 30 reads almost like English. The equivalent in a general-purpose language would be loops, conditions, and accumulator lists. More powerful, much noisier.
External vs. internal DSLs
Here’s the distinction that unlocks everything else in this article.
External DSLs are standalone languages. SQL, regex, and CSS each have their own syntax, their own parser, their own rules. Someone wrote a compiler or interpreter just for them. Building one is a serious engineering effort.
Internal DSLs (also called embedded DSLs) take a shortcut: instead of inventing a new language, you bend an existing general-purpose language until valid code in that language reads like a special-purpose one. There is no new parser, no new compiler. Every line is ordinary code — it just doesn’t look like it.
When people say “Kotlin DSL,” they mean an internal DSL written in Kotlin. This single fact dissolves most of the mystery:
A Kotlin DSL is not a new language. It is regular Kotlin code, written with language features that make it read like a configuration format.
Which raises the obvious question: what are those features?

Part 2: The Five Features That Make Kotlin a DSL Machine
Some languages are good hosts for internal DSLs; some are terrible. Kotlin happens to be one of the best, and it’s not an accident — JetBrains designed these features with DSLs explicitly in mind. There are five of them, and the fifth is the one that does the heavy lifting.
Feature 1: Trailing lambda syntax
In Kotlin, if the last parameter of a function is a lambda, you can move it outside the parentheses:
// These are the same call:
repeat(3, {
println("Hi")
})repeat(3) {
println("Hi")
}
And if the lambda is the only parameter, you can drop the parentheses entirely:
// These are the same call:
run({
println("Hi")
})run {
println("Hi")
}
Pause on that second example. run { ... } doesn't look like a function call. It looks like a language keyword. Like if or while with a block. That illusion is the foundation of every Kotlin DSL. When you see this in your Gradle file:
dependencies {
// ...
}…you are looking at a function named dependencies being called with a single lambda argument. That's it. The mystery of Gradle syntax is already half solved.
Feature 2: Extension functions
Kotlin lets you add functions to types you don’t own:
fun String.shout() = this.uppercase() + "!!!""hello".shout() // "HELLO!!!"
String is a final class from the standard library, yet we just gave it a new method. (Under the hood, it compiles to a static function taking the string as a parameter, so no class is actually modified.) For DSLs, this means you can attach domain vocabulary onto any type, including types from libraries you don't control.
If you’re an Android developer, you already own a mountain of these: the KTX libraries (core-ktx, fragment-ktx, and friends) are essentially this feature productized; extension functions that wrap Java Android APIs in idiomatic Kotlin, so you write view.isVisible = true instead of view.visibility = View.VISIBLE.
KTX and DSLs are cousins from the same toolbox with different ambitions:

• KTX makes single calls nicer;

• a DSL uses receivers (Feature 5, coming up) to build nested structure.
The line blurs in spots — sharedPreferences.edit { putString("key", value) } from core-ktx is a lambda with receiver, a one-block mini-DSL.
And to defuse a confusion this article exists to settle: KTX has nothing to do with .kts — one is a library naming convention, the other is a file extension for Kotlin scripts, which we'll get to in Part 3.
Feature 3: Infix functions
Functions marked infix can be called without the dot and parentheses:
infix fun Int.times(str: String) = str.repeat(this)3 times "Hey " // "Hey Hey Hey "
You’ve used this without noticing: to in mapOf("a" to 1) is not syntax. It's an infix extension function on generic types that returns a Pair.
Test frameworks lean on this hard. Kotest's result shouldBe 42 is an infix function call.
Feature 4: Operator overloading
Kotlin lets you define what +, -, [], (), and friends mean for your types, via functions with the operator keyword:
operator fun File.plus(other: File): File = File(this, other.name)Gradle’s Kotlin DSL uses this in places like sourceSets["main"]. That bracket access is an operator fun get(name: String) call on a collection-like container.
Feature 5: Lambdas with receivers — the big one
This is the feature. If you understand this section, you understand Kotlin DSLs. Read it twice if you need to; everything after depends on it.
A normal lambda that takes a parameter looks like this:
val greet: (StringBuilder) -> Unit = { sb ->
sb.append("Hello")
sb.append(", world")
}Inside the lambda, we must go through the parameter sb to do anything (or it if you didn’t specify a name for the param). Now look at the lambda with receiver version:
val greet: StringBuilder.() -> Unit = {
append("Hello")
append(", world")
}Two things changed:

• The type went from (StringBuilder) -> Unit to StringBuilder.() -> Unit. Read that as "a function on StringBuilder" rather than "a function taking a StringBuilder". An anonymous extension function.

• And inside the lambda, the parameter vanished. We call append(…) directly instead of sb.append(…)
Why does that work? Because inside a lambda with receiver, this is the receiver object. The lambda's body executes as if it were a method of StringBuilder. Just like you don't write this.append() inside a class's own methods, you don't write it here. The receiver is implicit.
Now combine this with trailing lambda syntax.
Here’s a tiny but complete DSL-style function. This is the pattern the standard library’s buildString uses:
// DSL Definition
fun buildString(builderAction: StringBuilder.() -> Unit): String {
val sb = StringBuilder()
sb.builderAction() // run the lambda with sb as the receiver
return sb.toString()
}// Call site
val message = buildString {
append("Hello")
append(", world")
}
Look at the call site. A block where you can “just say” append(...) and it works. No object in sight, no dots, no boilerplate. It feels like a special mini-language for string building. It is three lines of ordinary Kotlin.
This is the entire trick. Every Kotlin DSL you have ever seen (Gradle, Ktor’s routing, kotlinx.html) is some arrangement of functions that accept lambdas with receivers, so that each { } block changes what this is, and therefore changes which functions you can "just say".
Jetpack Compose layers more on top. A compiler plugin, recomposition, the @Composable machinery. But its scoped blocks, like RowScope deciding where weight() is legal, are this exact receiver trick.
And you’ve been using it all along without the DSL label:
the standard library’s scope functions (apply, run, with) are miniature DSLs built on nothing else. person.apply { name = "Ioannis" } is just a lambda with person as its receiver.
Nested blocks are just nested receivers:
html { // 'this' is an Html object → you can call body()
body { // 'this' is a Body object → you can call p()
p { // 'this' is a P object → you can call text fns
+"Hello"
}
}
}Each level of nesting swaps in a new implicit receiver, which swaps in a new vocabulary. That’s how a DSL gets structure: the type system itself decides what’s allowed where.
Write body { body { } } and it won't compile. Body has no body() function. Your "language" has grammar rules, enforced by the compiler, for free.
We’ll build exactly this HTML DSL in Part 5. But first, let’s cash in our new knowledge on the file that brought you here.

Part 3: What .kts Files Actually Are
Time to demystify the file extension.
.kt is a Kotlin source file. It contains declarations ( classes, functions, properties), and is compiled as part of a module: an app, a library, a test suite, a Gradle plugin.
A .kt file never runs by itself; it becomes part of something that's built, and if that something is an executable program, it needs an entry point like main().
.kts is a Kotlin script. The "s" stands for script. A script file can contain top-level executable statements. Code that just runs, top to bottom, no main() required:
// hello.kts — this whole file is valid as-is
val name = "Ioannis"println("Hello, $name")
println("Today is ${java.time.LocalDate.now()}")
If you have the Kotlin compiler installed, you can run it directly:
kotlinc -script hello.ktsThink of .kts as Kotlin's answer to a shell script or a Python file: a file you execute, not a file you build into something.
Kotlin scripting also lets a host application embed and run scripts, controlling what those scripts can see and do… Which brings us to Gradle.
So what is build.gradle.kts?
Gradle is a build tool, and your build configuration needs to be expressive: conditionals, loops, variables, logic.
So rather than using a static format like JSON or XML, Gradle made its config files executable scripts. For years those were written in Groovy (build.gradle); since Gradle 5.0 you can write them in Kotlin instead build.gradle.kts, a Kotlin script that Gradle compiles and executes when it configures your build.

Get Ioannis Anifantakis’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

And here’s the part almost no tutorial says out loud:
build.gradle.kts is a Kotlin script that executes with a Gradle Project object as its implicit receiver.
The whole file behaves like one giant lambda with a receiver; exactly the mechanism from Part 2, applied at file scale. Inside
